title: China's Rise in Premium Aquaculture
content:
Caviar and foie gras were once luxuries that many Chinese families could taste only when someone brought them back from abroad. That era is over. Today, China is the world’s largest producer and exporter of caviar, while its foie gras output accounts for roughly 45 percent of the global total. The shift, however, is about more than just scale. China already accounts for over 60 percent of global aquaculture output, but a more telling change lies in what it farms and how. Thanks to advances in aquaculture technology, species once associated with distant waters are now being raised in Chinese lakes, reservoirs, and even in the waters of saline-alkali land.

Salmon and trout are a prime example of this domestic transformation. Although China lacks the cold coastal waters that make Norway ideal for Atlantic salmon farming, inland regions such as Qinghai Province and Xinjiang Uygur Autonomous Region have glacier-fed water bodies. These environments are highly suitable for raising triploid rainbow trout, a larger, sterile variety developed through selective breeding. The rapid growth of domestic rainbow trout farming has coincided with rising demand, as health awareness increases and more consumers seek high-quality protein. In the first quarter of 2026, China emerged as the world’s second-largest market for salmon and trout consumption.

Eel production tells a similar story of domestic adaptation and growth. Fujian and Guangdong provinces now lead the large-scale farming of Japanese eel. What began partly as a response to foreign demand has become part of China’s own appetite for premium protein and rich flavor. Furthermore, technology is pushing the frontier of aquaculture even further. Automated feeding systems can now adjust rations according to water temperature and distribute feed evenly, which helps reduce injuries when fish compete for food.
